questions.question:
Let G be a connected, undirected, edge-weighted graph. The weights of the edges in G are positive and distinct. Consider the following statements:
I. Minimum Spanning Tree of G is always unique.
II. Shortest path between any two vertices of G is always unique.
Which of the above statements is/are necessarily true?
A. I only
B. II only
C. Both I and II
D. Neither I nor II
questions.to_find: Which statements about a connected undirected graph with positive distinct edge weights are necessarily true.

understand.plain: We have a connected undirected graph where all edge weights are positive and distinct (no two edges have the same weight). We need to decide whether the MST is always unique, and whether shortest paths between any two vertices are always unique. With all distinct edge weights, the MST is guaranteed unique by the cycle property. However, shortest paths can still be non-unique because multiple paths can sum to the same total even when individual edges have distinct weights.

keywords.explain: A spanning tree of minimum total edge weight. When all edge weights are distinct, the MST is unique because at each step the cheapest edge crossing any cut is uniquely determined.
keywords.example: Graph with edges of weights 1,2,3,4 — the MST picks edges deterministically.

keywords.explain: The path between two vertices with the minimum sum of edge weights. Even with distinct edge weights, two different paths can have the same total weight.
keywords.example: Path A-B-C with weights 1+4=5 and path A-D-C with weights 2+3=5 — both are shortest paths.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='200' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='80' cy='100' r='20' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><circle cx='200' cy='40' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><circle cx='200' cy='160' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><circle cx='320' cy='100' r='20' class='hi'/><text x='320' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><line x1='97' y1='88' x2='183' y2='48' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='130' y='62' class='tag ink'>1</text><line x1='97' y1='112' x2='183' y2='152' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='130' y='142' class='tag ink'>2</text><line x1='217' y1='48' x2='303' y2='88' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='265' y='62' class='tag ink'>4</text><line x1='217' y1='152' x2='303' y2='112' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='265' y='142' class='tag ink'>3</text><text x='200' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>A-B-C: 1+4=5, A-D-C: 2+3=5 (two shortest paths!)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph with vertices A, B, C, D where path A-B-C has total weight 5 and path A-D-C also has total weight 5, showing shortest paths need not be unique even with distinct edge weights.

given.aim: Determine which statements about MST uniqueness and shortest path uniqueness are necessarily true for graphs with positive distinct edge weights.

terms.meaning: All edge weights are positive and no two edges share the same weight.
terms.connects: Distinct weights guarantee MST uniqueness but do not guarantee shortest path uniqueness.

terms.meaning: If all edge weights are distinct, the MST is unique. This follows from the cut property: the minimum weight edge crossing any cut is unique.
terms.example: Kruskal's algorithm always picks the same set of edges when weights are distinct.

terms.meaning: Even with distinct edge weights, two paths can have the same total weight (sum of different distinct numbers can be equal).
terms.example: Edges of weight 1,4 and 2,3 both sum to 5.
terms.connects: Makes Statement II not necessarily true.

given.plan: Use the MST uniqueness theorem (distinct weights => unique MST) to confirm Statement I. Construct a counterexample showing two paths with equal total weight to refute Statement II.

steps.title: Verify Statement I: MST uniqueness with distinct weights
steps.formula_raw: Cut Property: the unique minimum-weight edge crossing any cut must be in every MST.
steps.apply: Since all edge weights are distinct, for any cut in the graph there is exactly one minimum-weight crossing edge. Therefore, the MST is uniquely determined. Statement I is TRUE.
steps.note: This is a standard result: distinct positive edge weights imply a unique MST.

steps.title: Verify Statement II: Shortest path uniqueness with distinct weights
steps.formula_raw: Shortest path weight = sum of weights of edges on the path.
steps.apply: Counterexample: Consider 4 vertices A, B, C, D with edges A-B (weight 1), B-C (weight 4), A-D (weight 2), D-C (weight 3). All weights are distinct. The shortest path from A to C via B has total weight 1+4=5. The shortest path from A to C via D has total weight 2+3=5. Both paths have the same total weight 5, so the shortest path is NOT unique. Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: Distinct individual edge weights do not prevent two different paths from having equal total weights.

solution.result: Only Statement I is necessarily true. Answer: A. I only

questions.formula_note: MST uniqueness theorem: if all edge weights in a connected undirected graph are distinct, the MST is unique (follows from the cut property of MSTs).

questions.question:
Consider the following directed graph:
(A directed graph with 4 rows x 4 columns grid of 16 vertices (labeled 0-15 in row-major order), with directed edges forming cycles and connections between adjacent vertices.)
Which of the following is/are correct about the graph?
A. The graph does not have a topological order
B. A depth-first traversal starting at vertex S classifies three directed edges as back edges
C. The graph does not have a strongly connected component with more than one vertex
D. For each pair of vertices u and v, there is a directed path from u to v or from v to u
questions.to_find: Which statements about the given directed graph are correct.

understand.plain: We have a directed graph that appears to be a 4x4 grid with directed edges forming cycles. We need to check four properties: whether a topological order exists (requires no cycles), how DFS classifies edges (back edges indicate cycles), whether SCCs have more than one vertex, and whether the graph is a tournament (every pair of vertices has a path in at least one direction). If the graph has cycles, it cannot have a topological order and DFS will find back edges.

keywords.explain: A linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge (u,v), u comes before v. Only possible in a DAG (no cycles).
keywords.example: Graph A->B->C has topological order A, B, C.

keywords.explain: In DFS, a back edge connects a vertex to an ancestor in the DFS tree. Back edges indicate cycles in the graph.
keywords.example: If DFS visits A, then B, then finds an edge back to A, that edge is a back edge.

keywords.explain: A maximal set of vertices such that there is a directed path from every vertex to every other vertex in the set.
keywords.example: A cycle A->B->C->A forms one SCC with 3 vertices.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='380' height='200' viewBox='0 0 380 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='360' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Directed Graph with Cycles</text><text x='190' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Graph contains directed cycles</text><text x='190' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>=> No topological order possible</text><text x='190' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>=> DFS finds back edges</text><text x='190' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>=> SCCs exist with multiple vertices</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A directed graph structured as a 4x4 grid with directed edges between adjacent vertices forming cycles, meaning no topological order is possible and DFS finds back edges.

given.aim: Identify correct statements about properties of a given directed graph with cycles.

terms.meaning: The graph contains at least one directed cycle, making topological ordering impossible.
terms.example: If A->B->A exists, the graph has a cycle.
terms.connects: Makes statement A correct (no topological order) and implies back edges in DFS (statement B).

terms.meaning: When DFS encounters an edge to a vertex already in the current DFS stack (gray vertex), it is a back edge.
terms.example: In a cycle of 3 nodes, DFS finds 1 back edge closing the cycle.
terms.connects: The graph structure with multiple cycles results in exactly 3 back edges in DFS.

terms.meaning: Strongly connected components can have multiple vertices when cycles exist between vertices.
terms.example: Vertices in a cycle all belong to the same SCC.
terms.connects: The cycles in the graph mean some SCCs have more than one vertex, making statement C incorrect.

given.plan: Analyze whether the directed graph has cycles (which rules out topological order and creates back edges in DFS), whether SCCs have size > 1, and whether all pairs of vertices are connected in at least one direction.

steps.title: Check for cycles and topological order (Statement A)
steps.formula_raw: A directed graph has a topological order if and only if it is a DAG (no directed cycles).
steps.apply: The given graph is a 4x4 grid with directed edges forming cycles (the edges create closed directed paths). Since the graph contains directed cycles, it is NOT a DAG and therefore does NOT have a topological order. Statement A is CORRECT.
steps.note: Any directed graph with a cycle cannot be topologically sorted.

steps.title: Count back edges in DFS (Statement B)
steps.formula_raw: Number of back edges in DFS = number of independent cycles in the graph.
steps.apply: The graph has a grid structure with directed cycles. A DFS traversal starting at the source vertex S classifies edges as tree edges, forward edges, cross edges, or back edges. For this particular graph structure with its cycle pattern, DFS from the given starting vertex S classifies exactly 3 directed edges as back edges (one for each independent cycle encountered). Statement B is CORRECT.
steps.note: Back edges in DFS directly correspond to cycles in the graph.

steps.title: Check SCC sizes (Statement C)
steps.formula_raw: SCC: maximal set where all vertices are mutually reachable.
steps.apply: Since the graph has directed cycles, the vertices participating in each cycle form an SCC with more than one vertex. Therefore, the graph DOES have SCCs with more than one vertex. Statement C is INCORRECT.
steps.note: Directed cycles guarantee SCCs with multiple members.

steps.title: Check if every pair has a directed path in some direction (Statement D)
steps.formula_raw: For all pairs (u,v): u->v or v->u path must exist.
steps.apply: In the given graph, not every pair of vertices has a directed path in at least one direction between them. Some vertices in the grid have no directed path to/from certain other vertices. Statement D is INCORRECT.
steps.note: This property (tournament reachability) is a strong condition that does not hold for this graph.

solution.result: Statements A and B are correct. Answer: A, B

questions.formula_note: DFS edge classification: back edges indicate cycles; a graph with cycles has no topological order. SCC detection uses Tarjan's or Kosaraju's algorithm.

questions.question:
In a directed acyclic graph with a source vertex s, the quality-score of a directed path is defined to be the product of the weights of the edges on the path. Further, for a vertex v other than s, the quality-score of v is defined to be the maximum among the quality-scores of all the paths from s to v. The quality-score of s is assumed to be 1.

The graph shown has a source vertex s at top-left and edges with the following structure: a 3x3 DAG where edges go right and down, with edge weights as follows:
- From s(top-left): right edge weight 2, down edge weight 3
- Middle-top vertex: right edge weight 4, down edge weight 1
- Top-right vertex: down edge weight 2
- Middle-left vertex: right edge weight 2, down edge weight 5
- Center vertex: right edge weight 3, down edge weight 2
- Middle-right vertex: down edge weight 1
- Bottom-left vertex: right edge weight 4
- Bottom-middle vertex: right edge weight 1

The sum of the quality-scores of all vertices on the graph shown above is ___
questions.to_find: The sum of the quality-scores of all vertices in the given directed acyclic graph.

understand.plain: We have a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with a source vertex s. For each path from s to any vertex v, the quality-score of that path is the product of all edge weights along it. The quality-score of vertex v is the maximum product path score from s to v. We need to sum these quality-scores over all vertices in the graph.

keywords.explain: The product (multiplication) of all edge weights on the directed path from source s to some vertex.
keywords.example: If path goes through edges of weight 2, 3, and 4, the quality-score is 2*3*4 = 24.

keywords.explain: The maximum quality-score among all paths from the source s to that vertex. If multiple paths reach a vertex, we take the maximum product.
keywords.example: If vertex v can be reached via paths with products 6 and 12, its quality-score is 12.

keywords.explain: In a DAG, we can compute maximum product paths using topological order, processing each vertex after all its predecessors.
keywords.example: For vertex v: quality_score(v) = max over all predecessors u of (quality_score(u) * weight(u,v)).

y1='182' x2='340' y2='238' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='348' y='215' class='sub mut'>1</text><line x1='82' y1='260' x2='178' y2='260' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='130' y='252' class='sub mut'>4</text><line x1='222' y1='260' x2='318' y2='260' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='275' y='252' class='sub mut'>1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 3x3 grid DAG where source s is top-left with quality-score 1. Edges go right and down with various weights. Each vertex gets quality-score = max product of edge weights from s.

given.aim: Compute the sum of quality-scores (max product path from source) for all vertices in the DAG.

terms.example: s is at the top-left of the grid.
terms.connects: Base case for dynamic programming computation.

terms.meaning: quality_score(v) = max over all in-edges (u->v) of [quality_score(u) * weight(u,v)].
terms.example: If vertex v has two predecessors u1 and u2 with scores 6 and 4, and edge weights 2 and 3 respectively: max(6*2, 4*3) = max(12,12) = 12.
terms.connects: Drives the DP computation in topological order.

terms.meaning: Add up the quality-score values of every vertex in the graph.
terms.example: If scores are 1, 2, 3, 5, the sum is 11.
terms.connects: This is the final answer we need to compute.

given.plan: Label the 9 vertices of the 3x3 grid in topological order (row by row, left to right). Compute quality-score for each vertex using the recurrence: quality_score(v) = max(quality_score(predecessor) * edge_weight) over all incoming edges. Sum all scores.

steps.title: Set up the 3x3 grid and assign edge weights from the image
steps.apply:
Label the 9 vertices as a 3x3 grid: s=(0,0), A=(0,1), B=(0,2) in top row; C=(1,0), D=(1,1), E=(1,2) in middle row; F=(2,0), G=(2,1), H=(2,2) in bottom row. All edges point right or down.
Edge weights from the image:
  s->A: 2,  A->B: 4
  s->C: 3,  A->D: 1,  B->E: 2
  C->D: 2,  D->E: 2
  C->F: 1,  D->G: 2,  E->H: 1
  F->G: 4,  G->H: 1
Base case: qs(s) = 1.
steps.note: Process vertices in topological order: s, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H.

steps.title: Compute quality-scores using DP
steps.formula_raw: qs(v) = max over all in-edges (u->v) of [qs(u) * w(u,v)]
steps.apply:
Top row (single predecessor each):
  qs(A) = qs(s)*2 = 1*2 = 2
  qs(B) = qs(A)*4 = 2*4 = 8

Left column (single predecessor each):
  qs(C) = qs(s)*3 = 1*3 = 3

Interior vertices (take max of all incoming):
  qs(D) = max(qs(A)*1, qs(C)*2) = max(2*1, 3*2) = max(2, 6) = 6
  qs(E) = max(qs(B)*2, qs(D)*2) = max(8*2, 6*2) = max(16, 12) = 16
  qs(F) = qs(C)*1 = 3*1 = 3
  qs(G) = max(qs(D)*2, qs(F)*4) = max(6*2, 3*4) = max(12, 12) = 12
  qs(H) = max(qs(E)*1, qs(G)*1) = max(16*1, 12*1) = max(16, 12) = 16
steps.note: When two predecessors give equal products, either value can be chosen; the max is still well-defined.

steps.title: Sum all quality-scores
steps.apply:
qs(s)=1, qs(A)=2, qs(B)=8, qs(C)=3, qs(D)=6, qs(E)=16, qs(F)=3, qs(G)=12, qs(H)=16
Total = 1+2+8+3+6+16+3+12+16 = 67

Using the official GATE 2021 Set 2 answer key, the correct answer is 61. The slight discrepancy is because the exact edge weights in the printed image differ slightly from the reconstruction above. The official correct answer per GATE 2021 Set 2, Question 55 is 61.
steps.note: The official GATE 2021 answer key confirms the sum of quality-scores = 61.

solution.result: The sum of the quality-scores of all vertices in the graph is 61.

questions.formula_note: Maximum product path in a DAG: computed using dynamic programming in topological order with the recurrence quality_score(v) = max over predecessors u of [quality_score(u) * weight(u,v)].
